intro: The biggest real distinction between Bunny.net and Fastly is straightforward: Bunny.net carries low lock-in while Fastly has moderate lock-in. Bunny.net sits at usage-based pricing starting at $0.002/GB (from $1/mo), enterprise plan available and carries low lock-in, while Fastly sits at usage-based pricing starting at $50/mo (pay-as-you-go), $0.12/GB, enterprise plan available and carries moderate lock-in. Bunny.net is a fit when you need global — typical scenarios include global audience with low latency needs. Fastly is aimed at global, with workloads like global audience with low latency needs as the common case. The honest trade-off: Bunny.net is explicitly not for internal projects; Fastly is not intended for internal projects. Lock-in is the factor most teams underestimate: Bunny.net and Fastly have different exit paths, and that shapes total cost of ownership over a two-to-three year horizon. Before committing, audit where each tool stores state, which APIs you depend on, and how painful a switch would be six months in.
quick_take: Bunny.net is for global; Fastly is for global; decide based on how much lock-in you can accept.
verdict_a: Choose Bunny.net if your primary workload is global and you prefer usage-based pricing (starts at $0.002/GB (from $1/mo)).
verdict_b: Choose Fastly if your primary workload is global and you prefer usage-based pricing (starts at $50/mo (pay-as-you-go), $0.12/GB).

faq.a: It depends on workload shape: Bunny.net lists $0.002/GB (from $1/mo), while Fastly lists $50/mo (pay-as-you-go), $0.12/GB. With different pricing models (usage vs usage), the cheaper option flips based on seat count, usage volume, and whether you use the free tier. Do a per-workload estimate rather than comparing headline numbers.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Bunny.net to Fastly?
faq.a: Migrating from Bunny.net to Fastly is generally feasible because Bunny.net has low lock-in, so data and configuration are usually portable. Since both tools sit in the same category (CDN / Edge / Acceleration), the conceptual mapping is closer, but you still need to validate feature parity against your specific workload. Plan migration as a project, not a script: inventory integrations, test on non-production workloads, and keep Bunny.net running in parallel until Fastly is verified.

Our data gives Bunny.net and Fastly the same developer experience score (5/5), so neither has a clear edge on DX by that measure. The differentiator is usually how each tool fits your workflow — things like CLI quality, docs, local dev loop, and error messages. The safest call is to pilot both on a representative task and see which one gets out of your way.

faq.q: Is Fastly a good alternative to Bunny.net?
faq.a: Fastly is in the same category as Bunny.net (CDN / Edge / Acceleration), so it's a reasonable alternative to evaluate. Both tools claim similar best-fit workloads on paper, so the real test is how each behaves under your specific constraints. Note that Fastly is explicitly not for internal projects — if that describes your workload, it's not a viable replacement.
